title: Live: First flight from Dubai to Sydney departs
author: Courtney Gould
fullText: The first flight from Dubai, bound for Sydney, has departed. Foreign Minister Penny Wong earlier told the ABC the flight would only take off if the airspace was considered safe. Follow all the updates in our live blog. Key Events15 minutes agoTue 3 Mar 2026 at 10:39pm41 minutes agoTue 3 Mar 2026 at 10:14pm45 minutes agoTue 3 Mar 2026 at 10:09pmKey Event15m agoTue 3 Mar 2026 at 10:39pmFirst flight from Dubai to Sydney departsA flight from Dubai to Sydney has departed the United Arab Emirates, according to flight tracking data.It's the first flight to depart the UAE for Australia since airspace was closed and travel was halted due to the conflict in the Middle East.Foreign Minister Penny Wong told ABC's AM this morning the flight would only take off if the circumstances were safe.It's due to arrive in Australia at 10.30pm local time. More than 30,000 people are tracking the flight on FlightRadar24. It's currently the most-watched plane worldwide.(Flight Radar 24)Key Event41m agoTue 3 Mar 2026 at 10:14pmTim Wilson says Jim Chalmers collects more excise when petrol prices surge. Is he right?The shadow treasurer has accused the government of cashing in on the prospect of higher petrol prices."Of course, excise means that when the price of petrol goes up, Jim Chalmers collects more money. So that's a matter for him to decide whether he stops his tax addiction or not," Tim Wilson says.That's not right. The fuel excise Wilson is referring to does not change when the price of petrol goes up.The government does collect a little more in GST when the price of petrol, or anything else subject to the tax, goes up.But that money goes straight to the states and territories. And it's not the excise.At any rate, Wilson doesn't say the fuel excise should be cut, as governments have done sometimes before during price spikes. He says it's something Chalmers can do if he wants to, but probably won't.Key Event45m agoTue 3 Mar 2026 at 10:09pmShadow treasurer Wilson floats cut to fuel exciseShould the government consider a cut in the fuel excise as a temporary measure amid the conflict in the Middle East?That's an option that Shadow Treasurer Tim Wilson floated this morning."One of the biggest components around the cost of petrol is tax. And of course, excise means that when the price of petrol goes up, Jim Chalmers collects more money. So that's a matter for him to decide whether he wants to stop his tax addiction or not," he told Sky News.The fuel excise is a flat sales tax (more on this shortly from friend of the blog Tom Crowley). Wilson was repeatedly asked if the Liberals were advocating for a cut in the excise."We're going through our processes to address how we're going to confront these realities."But it seems pretty clear to me that the treasurer could take that action if he chose to take that action. At this point, he’s quite happy to take extra money from tax revenue from Australians because he’s got a credit card addiction that he needs to pay off."1h agoTue 3 Mar 2026 at 9:29pm'All responsible': Former Liberal MP urges party to heed lessons of campaign reviewFormer Liberal MP Keith Wolahan has told the ABC he is "more likely than not" to run as the Liberal candidate for Menzies in the 2028 federal election, pending the support of his party."These are decisions for the Liberal Party. We don't just get to say we're running; we have to earn the trust of our party, but I'm more likely than not to run," he said.Wolahan lost the safe Liberal seat of Menzies — named after the party's founder — to Labor in 2025 and has previously indicated he is "open" to contesting the next election."We're all collectively responsible … I was the member, I lost the seat, and I take full responsibility for that."His comments come after a secret review into the Liberal's disastrous campaign was made public by the prime minister, who tabled the report during Question Time.Its authors, Liberal Party elders Pru Goward and Nick Minchin, found a "notable absence of reflection" from key figures involved in orchestrating campaign strategy and policy.Among key "mistakes" identified by the review was deputy liberal leader Jane Hume's comments that "Chinese spies" were helping the Labor Party, which Wolahan has partly blamed for the loss of his seat, home to a large Chinese diaspora."I haven't said anything about that publicly … Jane Hume is someone I respect greatly and is an excellent deputy leader," Wolahan said."She made a mistake; she's acknowledged that mistake. The problem for the party is how do we learn from this? And the lesson is, we need to be sensitive about how things sound or can be weaponised by the Labor Party."Wolahan also urged his party to refocus on urban voters — including multicultural Australians, women, and young people — but says he backs Angus Taylor's vision for the Liberals, including his rhetoric on immigration that "numbers are too high, and standards are too low.""I think that is one way to talk about social cohesion. I think Angus Taylor is right to have a conversation about making sure the system is making sure [prospective migrants] have the right to come here and call themselves Australians."Key Event1h agoTue 3 Mar 2026 at 9:13pm'Boss move' for PM to table Liberal review: Hastie(ABC News: Matt Roberts)There was a bit of dramatic flair from the prime minister QT yesterday, as Anthony Albanese tabled the leaked review of the Liberal Party's election loss.The party's executive last week decided to shelve the review to keep it private. Which means, of course, it was immediately sent to media outlets.Andrew Hastie laughed that it was a bit of a "boss move" for the PM to table it (and therefore make it public)."He likes theatre. He had a twinkle in his eye when he did that," Hastie says."Obviously, it was leaked, and there was a bit of stagecraft from him … But we're not going to live in the past, we're going to live in the future, and our mission is to restore Australian standard of living."1h agoTue 3 Mar 2026 at 9:07pmLiberal frontbencher Hastie argues we're in a 'new stage of history'Liberal frontbencher Andrew Hastie yesterday made the argument that he doesn't think the global rules based order exists anymore.Speaking with ABC's Radio National Breakfast the new shadow industry minister says we're in a "new stage of history" where the construct of  international institutions is "defunct in a sense"."We've got a different president who is mercantilist when it comes to his sort of economics. I think he's an apex opportunist. We've seen him strike Nigeria, Venezuela, he's attempting regime change now in Iran," he says."But we've also seen the rise of autocracies, China's flexing its muscles — we've seen Russia invade Ukraine illegally and immorally, and Iran, of course, was sponsoring Hamas, Hezbollah and the Houthis in the Middle East."1h agoTue 3 Mar 2026 at 8:59pmAustralia should distance itself from US, Israel action in Iran: WilkieThe government has repeatedly said Australia is not an outlier in the approach it has taken towards the US and Israel's strikes on Iran.But independent MP Andrew Wilkie doesn't think Australia's view should be positioned on what other nations think or say."This is an opportunity for Australia to take a principled position and say there is no justification on the basis of self-defence by the US at this time," he told ABC's Radio National Breakfast. "They should take the matter to the United Nations and try to win the support of the United Nations. Until that is done, then this is, in international law, an illegal war, and we should distance ourselves from it."Wilkie adds Australia is not "entirely dislocated" from this war because "there's no doubt that US intelligence and targeting information and other information that will be flowing through Pine Gap."Key Event2h agoTue 3 Mar 2026 at 8:53pm'Alarming parallels' between now and Iraq war in 2003: Wilkie(ABC News: Matt Roberts)Andrew Wilkie, a former Australian intelligence officer turned independent MP, stopped by the ABC Radio National Breakfast studio.Wilkie resigned from the Office of National Assessment before the 2003 invasion of Iraq because he was concerned about the intelligence justifying the action.He says he sees "alarming parallels between what's happening now and what happened in 2003"."In both cases, the threat was exaggerated to justify the war. Let's not forget that for a country to attack another country in self-defence, then they have to have been attacked first," he says."The other parallel is that in both cases, there was a failure to secure the approval of the United Nations. In fact, this time around, the United States hasn't even attempted to get the approval of the United Nations."Finally, he notes that this is taking place at a time when the Republicans' "polling is poor" and that congressional midterms are scheduled for later this year.2h agoTue 3 Mar 2026 at 8:47pm'Relatively small' volume of trade hit by Middle East war, says minister(ABC News: Matt Roberts)Trade Minister Don Farrell is downplaying the impact of the Middle East war on Australia's trade.Speaking with ABC's News Breakfast he says only a "relatively small" number of Australian exports go through the region.He'll be meeting with Australia's 40 largest organisations today to discuss the situation."A relatively small amount, about $15b worth of trade [flows through the Middle East]. Obviously, that's very important for those companies that are trading there," he said.Farrell says Australia's trade with the region is increasing and pointed to a free trade agreement with the United Arab Emirates."Already, our beef trade has doubled in the six months that the trade agreement has been in operation. But of course, all of that gets affected by this uncertainty of the war in the Middle East," he says.Asked how long he thought the conflict would go on for, Farrell wouldn't speculate.2h agoTue 3 Mar 2026 at 8:34pmWong dodges question about legality of US, Israel strikesForeign Minister Penny Wong has again sidestepped the question about the legality of the US and Israel's strikes on Iran.She reiterates that it is a matter for the two nations.Pushed on the statement from the US President Donald Trump overnight that he wanted to preempt a strike by Iran on Israel, Wong doesn't engage."The legal basis of these strikes is ultimately a matter for the United States and ... Israel," she says.Key Event2h agoTue 3 Mar 2026 at 8:23pmFlight scheduled to depart Dubai for Sydney this morning: WongForeign Minister Penny Wong says there is a flight scheduled to depart Dubai for Sydney this morning."But obviously that is dependent on the circumstances," she told ABC's AM. Wong urged people on the ground who were stuck in transit to stay in contact with their airlines because flights have been cancelled and changed at short notice.The government is also looking at "all contingencies" to get Australians out of the region, but it's a geographically challenging task.Asked about options out of the UAE and Qatar transport hubs via land to Riyadh or Muscat."I'd make the point that there have been attacks on Saudi Arabia. It is one of the 10 countries Iran has now attacked in the region. This is a much broader spread of conflict in the Middle East than we have seen," she says. Would the government consider additional chartered flights to get Australians out?All contingencies are on the table but Wong says with he scale of Australians in the region the best way to get everyone out is via commercial flights, when they're able to get into the air.2h agoTue 3 Mar 2026 at 8:17pmMore than 24,000 Australians in Middle East, Wong describes challenge as 'consular crisis'Foreign Minister Penny Wong has just dialled into ABC's AM, where she has been asked about the latest advice to Australians in the Middle East."Obviously, this is a perilous and unpredictable time in the Middle East, and we are very conscious of how challenging, how distressing this is for Australians who are in the Middle East and for friends and family overseas," she says.She says most of the Australians in the region are in the UAE."This is a consular crisis that dwarfs ...  any that Australia has had to deal with in terms of the numbers of people."2h agoTue 3 Mar 2026 at 8:07pmConcerns over what protracted Middle East conflict could mean for Australian economyHealth Minister Mark Butler was out and about early this morning, discussing the government's response to the conflict in the Middle East.The Labor frontbencher acknowledged the concern about the conflict continuing for some time."Of course all of us are nervous about this becoming a very long, protracted war because we know from previous experience when there's a protracted war in the Middle East, it reverberates right through the global economy," he told Nine.It comes amid concerns about Australia's fuel stockpiles. The treasurer wrote to the competition watchdog yesterday, tasking it with monitoring petrol prices for signs of price gouging.Butler says the government is confident it has enough fuel to last through May.Key Event2h agoTue 3 Mar 2026 at 8:03pmAlbanese speaks to UAE president, NZ PMPrime Minister Anthony Albanese spoke with the president of the United Arab Emirates to discuss the escalating war in the Middle East.Albanese thanked Mohammed Bin Zayed Al Nahyan for the care and hospitality provided by the UAE to Australians stranded by the conflict.According to the federal government, an estimated 24,000 Australians remain in the UAE, with that figure including residents and those in transit. In a separate conversation, Albanese also spoke with his NZ counterpart, Christopher Luxon,to discuss the consular challenges arising from the war.2h agoTue 3 Mar 2026 at 7:58pmGood morning👋Hi pals. Welcome back to another day in federal politics.I'm Courtney Gould from the ABC's Parliament House bureau here and ready to guide you through the day.Prime Minister Anthony Albanese spoke with the president of the United Arab Emirates last night as the conflict in the Middle East continues into a fourth day. Expect there still to be plenty of domestic reaction.Meanwhile back at home there will be some shift in focus towards the economy ahead of the release of the national accounts data today.I'm hanging out for a coffee, so grab one for me and settle in.
summary: The first flight from Dubai to Sydney has departed the UAE after the conflict in the Middle East halted air travel. Follow live.
